title: UrbanFleet Rush Hour Meltdown: The 502 Storm

personality.demeanor: pragmatic and numbers-driven, tracks 502 error rate obsessively
personality.recurring_concern: delivery drivers stranded mid-route and customers waiting for packages

facts: 1,200 drivers on the road with no way to get updated delivery manifests
packages piling up at distribution centers
400 grocery deliveries scheduled in next two hours, perishable goods, time-sensitive
if drivers cannot be routed, Metro Market will invoke SLA penalty clause
Auto Scaling launched two new instances five minutes ago, already being deregistered from target group
instances come up healthy at OS level but load balancer keeps pulling them out
something wrong with how health is being checked
instances pass EC2 status checks and application starts correctly
problem is between the load balancer and the instances
look at how the ALB determines whether a target is healthy

hints.hint: ALB returning 502 means it cannot get a valid response from backend instances -- why would healthy instances fail the load balancer's health check?
ALB health checks are configured in the target group -- check the health check path and what response it expects
compare the health check configuration (path, port, expected status code, thresholds) to what the application actually serves
health check configured for port 8080 but application listens on port 3000 -- every check fails because nothing listens on 8080, so ALB deregisters every instance

glossary.ALB (Application Load Balancer): A load balancer that distributes incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests across multiple EC2 instances. It sits between users and your servers so no single server gets overwhelmed.
glossary.Target Group: A named set of EC2 instances (or other resources) that an ALB sends traffic to. You register instances into a target group and the ALB checks their health before routing requests.
glossary.Health Check: A periodic test the ALB runs against each registered instance to decide whether that instance is working. If an instance fails enough consecutive checks, the ALB stops sending it traffic.
glossary.502 Bad Gateway: An HTTP error code the ALB returns when it has no healthy backend instance to forward a request to, or the backend sent an invalid response.
glossary.Deregistration: The process of removing an instance from a target group. When an instance fails health checks, the ALB deregisters it so it no longer receives traffic.
glossary.Auto Scaling Group: A collection of EC2 instances that AWS can automatically grow or shrink based on demand or health status. It launches replacements when instances are terminated.
glossary.traffic-port: A special value for the health check port setting that tells the ALB to check the same port the instance is registered on, rather than a hard-coded port number.
glossary.Health Check Threshold: The number of consecutive successful (or failed) health checks required before the ALB changes an instance's status from unhealthy to healthy, or vice versa.
glossary.CloudWatch Metric: A time-series data point published by AWS services. For ALBs, metrics like HTTPCode_ELB_502_Count and HealthyHostCount track error rates and target health over time.
glossary.Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Managing server and service configuration through code files (like Terraform or CloudFormation) instead of clicking through the AWS console by hand.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player sees ALB online but every response is 502, target group shows zero healthy instances despite EC2 status checks passing
narrative_arc.trials: new instances launched by Auto Scaling also fail within seconds, creating churn cycle -- health check port does not match application port
narrative_arc.revelation: health check changed to port 8080 by IaC update two hours earlier, application still listens on port 3000, every check times out
narrative_arc.return: update health check port back to traffic-port, instances healthy within 10 seconds, add dedicated /health endpoint and pre-deployment port-match validation

components.name: Application Load Balancer (urbanfleet-prod)
components.role: Receives all inbound HTTPS traffic on port 443 and forwards requests to healthy targets in the target group
components.failure_impact: If the ALB has no healthy targets, all requests return 502 Bad Gateway errors

components.name: Target Group (urbanfleet-dispatch-tg)
components.role: Registers EC2 instances on port 3000 and runs health checks on port 8080 to determine target health
components.failure_impact: If health checks fail, instances are deregistered and the ALB has no targets to forward traffic to

components.name: EC2 Instances (t3.large, 4 instances across us-east-1a and us-east-1b)
components.role: Run the dispatch application on port 3000, serving driver routing and delivery manifest requests
components.failure_impact: If instances are deregistered due to failed health checks, drivers cannot receive updated delivery manifests

components.name: Auto Scaling Group (urbanfleet-dispatch-asg)
components.role: Maintains desired capacity of 4 instances (min 2, max 8) with ELB health check type
components.failure_impact: If ASG detects ELB-unhealthy instances, it terminates and replaces them, creating a churn cycle when health checks are misconfigured

components.name: RDS PostgreSQL (urbanfleet-db, Multi-AZ)
components.role: Backend database storing dispatch data, driver routes, and delivery manifests
components.failure_impact: If the database is unreachable, the dispatch application cannot serve any data

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects metrics for HTTPCode_ELB_502_Count, HealthyHostCount, and UnHealthyHostCount
components.failure_impact: If metrics are not monitored, health check misconfigurations go undetected until users report errors

system_narration.data_flow: Driver apps and the dispatch dashboard send HTTPS requests to the ALB on port 443. The ALB forwards requests to healthy instances in the target group on port 3000. Each instance runs the dispatch service, which reads and writes to the RDS database. The ALB health-checks each instance every 10 seconds on the configured health check port. If 3 consecutive checks fail, the instance is marked unhealthy and removed from rotation.
system_narration.what_broke: An IaC update changed the target group health check port from traffic-port (3000) to 8080. Nothing listens on port 8080, so every health check times out after 5 seconds. After 3 consecutive failures (30 seconds), each instance is deregistered. With zero healthy targets, the ALB returns 502 for every request. Auto Scaling detects the ELB-unhealthy instances, terminates them, and launches replacements -- which also fail the same health check, creating a continuous churn cycle.

resolution.root_cause: The ALB target group health check is configured to check port 8080, but the application listens on port 3000. A recent infrastructure change updated the health check port from 3000 to 8080 without updating the application. Every health check fails, causing the ALB to deregister all instances as unhealthy and return 502 errors to clients.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the ALB health check port (8080) does not match the application port (3000)
Player understands that failed health checks cause the ALB to deregister instances, resulting in 502 errors
Player proposes updating the target group health check to use port 3000 (or traffic-port) to match the application

learning_objectives: ALB health checks determine which instances receive traffic -- misconfigured health checks cause healthy instances to be deregistered
502 Bad Gateway from an ALB means it has no healthy targets to forward the request to
Target group health check settings (port, path, thresholds) must match the actual application configuration
sop_steps: Open the EC2 console and go to Target Groups. A target group is a named collection of servers that a load balancer sends traffic to. Select urbanfleet-dispatch-tg.

Click the Health checks tab. The load balancer periodically checks each server to see if it is working. Note the health check port (8080) and path (/health). The port is where the load balancer sends its test request, and the path is the URL it checks.
Compare the health check port (8080) to the port the application actually listens on (3000). If these do not match, the health check is knocking on a door nobody is behind, and every check will fail.

Fix the mismatch. Click Edit health check settings and change the port from 8080 to 'traffic-port'. The traffic-port setting tells the load balancer to check the same port the server is registered on, so it always matches the application.
Save the change. The load balancer will re-check each server within 10-30 seconds. No server restart is needed.

Go to the Targets tab and verify that all servers transition from 'unhealthy' to 'healthy'. This means the load balancer can now reach the application and will start sending it real user traffic again.
Confirm the 502 errors have stopped. Check the ALB access logs or the HTTPCode_ELB_502_Count metric in CloudWatch (AWS's monitoring service). The 502 count should drop to zero.

Fix the root cause in code. Find the infrastructure-as-code template (Terraform, CloudFormation, etc.) that changed the health check port and revert it to traffic-port. This prevents the problem from coming back on the next deployment.

Add a pre-deployment check to your CI/CD pipeline that verifies the health check port matches the port the application listens on. This catches misconfigured health checks before they reach production.

Create a dedicated /health endpoint in your application. A good health endpoint checks that downstream services (like the database and cache) are reachable, so the load balancer knows the server is truly ready to serve traffic, not just running.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm on HTTPCode_ELB_502_Count with a threshold of 10 per minute and a 1-minute evaluation period. This alerts your team within a minute if the load balancer starts returning errors to users.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Health check path returns non-200 status
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The port is correct, but the URL path the load balancer checks (for example, /health) returns an error response (like 500 or 404) because the health endpoint is broken or does not exist at that path. The load balancer reports a different failure reason -- Target.ResponseCodeMismatch (meaning the response code was not the expected 200 OK) instead of Target.Timeout (meaning no response at all).
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always deploy your health check endpoint before telling the target group to use it. After deployment, manually test the endpoint to confirm it returns a 200 OK response.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Security group blocks health check traffic
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The health check port and path are correct, but the server's firewall (security group) does not allow incoming traffic from the load balancer on the health check port. The symptom looks identical to a port mismatch -- checks time out -- but the fix is different. Instead of changing the health check settings, you need to add a firewall rule that lets the load balancer talk to the server.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Make sure the server's security group allows inbound traffic from the load balancer's security group on both the application port and the health check port.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Health check timeout shorter than application startup time
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The port and path are correct, but the application takes a long time to start up (loading data, warming caches, etc.). The load balancer checks too soon and marks the server as unhealthy before the application has finished starting. Unlike a port mismatch, the servers would eventually pass health checks if given enough time.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the Auto Scaling health check grace period (a waiting period before health checks count) to be longer than the worst-case startup time for your application. You can also increase the unhealthy threshold -- the number of failed checks required before marking a server unhealthy -- to give slow starters more chances.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Target group registered on wrong port
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The health check uses traffic-port (which automatically matches the registered port), but the servers were registered on the wrong port in the first place (for example, 8080 instead of 3000). Both real traffic and health checks go to the wrong port. The symptom looks similar to this scenario, but the fix is re-registering the servers on the correct port rather than changing the health check.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Verify that the port in your target group registration matches the port your application actually listens on. Check this in your launch template or infrastructure-as-code configuration.

sop_practices: Always set the health check port to 'traffic-port' (which automatically matches the port the server is registered on) unless you have a dedicated health check endpoint on a separate port that you have confirmed is working. Hard-coding a port number is fragile and easy to get wrong.

Treat any change to health check settings as a high-risk deployment. Test in staging first, deploy gradually (with a canary -- a small test group), and immediately watch the HealthyHostCount metric after rollout to catch problems before all servers are affected.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm that fires when HealthyHostCount (the number of servers the load balancer considers healthy) drops below your minimum acceptable number. This catches health check problems before they cause 502 errors for users -- it is an earlier warning signal than monitoring 502 counts alone.

When using Auto Scaling with load balancer health checks, set the health check grace period (the waiting time before health checks start counting) long enough for your application to fully start up. If the grace period is too short, Auto Scaling will keep terminating servers before they finish booting, creating an endless cycle of launching and killing instances.
